content: The correct order of reactivity of halogens with alkanes is :
explanation:
The correct order is

$$\mathrm{F}_2>\mathrm{Cl}_2>\mathrm{Br}_2>\mathrm{I}_2$$

(Option A).

Why?

Bond‐dissociation energy (BDE):

• F–F is weakest → easiest to homolytically cleave → most reactive

• Cl–Cl stronger than F–F but weaker than Br–Br → intermediate reactivity

• Br–Br still stronger → lower reactivity

• I–I is strongest among these or gives endothermic substitution → effectively unreactive

Reaction enthalpy with a C–H bond becomes less exothermic down the group, so reactivity decreases in the same order.

content: Choose the incorrect statement from the following.

options.content: $\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ is widely used as a propellant in aerosols.
DDT was widely used because of its effectiveness against mosquitos that spread malaria
Freon 1, 2 is manufactured from trichloromethane by Swartz reaction
Antiseptic property of Iodoform is due to the liberation of $I_2$ when it comes in contact with skin

explanation:
The incorrect statement is Option C.

Explanation:

Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane, CCl₂F₂) is made by fluorinating carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄), not trichloromethane (CHCl₃).

• Swarts reaction (halogen exchange) example:

$$\mathrm{CCl_4} + 2\,\mathrm{HF}\;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{SbCl_5}}\;\mathrm{CCl_2F_2} + 2\,\mathrm{HCl}$$

By contrast:

• Option A: Methylene chloride (CH₂Cl₂) has indeed been used as an aerosol propellant/solvent.

• Option B: DDT was widely employed because it effectively killed malaria-carrying mosquitoes.

• Option D: Iodoform’s antiseptic action comes from slow release of I₂ on contact with skin.

content: Identify the incorrect statement.

options.content: When (一)-2-bromooctane is reacted with aqueous KOH , the substitution follows $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ mechanism and product formed is (一) -Octan-2-ol.
Benzylic halides show higher reactivity towards $S_N 1$ reaction.
The reaction of $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHCl}-\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5$ with alc. KOH on heating yields two products.
Optically active (-)-2-Methylbutan-1-ol on reaction with HCl yields a product $(+)$-1-Chloro-2-methylbutane.

explanation:
The statement in Option A is incorrect.

– In an SN2 reaction the configuration at the stereogenic centre is inverted.

– Starting from (–)-2-bromooctane, attack by OH⁻ inverts the configuration, giving (+)-2-octanol, not (–)-2-octanol.

content: Identify the final product $[Z]$ formed when Chlorobenzene undergoes the given series of reactions:
Which one of the following is a true statement with reference to reaction between 2- Bromo-2-methylpropane and aqueous KOH ?

options.content: The rate of reaction depends on the concentration of the haloalkane and the nucleophile $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
The reaction occurs at a fast rate since the substrate is a tertiary alkyl halide and follows $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism.
The reaction is not favoured by the presence of polar protic solvent.
The reaction occurs at a slow rate since it follows $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ mechanism.

explanation:
Option B.

Explanation:

2-Bromo-2-methylpropane is a tertiary alkyl halide.

In aqueous KOH (a polar protic medium), it undergoes an $$\mathrm{S_N1}$$ mechanism:

Rate-determining step is ionization to form a tertiary carbocation.

Tertiary carbocations are especially stabilized by inductive and hyperconjugation effects, so this step (and thus the overall reaction) is fast.

Polar protic solvents further stabilize both the carbocation and the leaving bromide ion, favoring $$\mathrm{S_N1}$$.

content: Which of the following haloalkanes will give more than one isomeric product, on being heated with alc. KOH ?
explanation:
2-Chlorobutane (Option D) is the only one that can eliminate a β-H from either side of the C–Cl bond, giving more than one alkene:

Removal of H from C-1 → CH₂=CH–CH₂–CH₃ (1-butene)

Removal of H from C-3 → CH₃–CH=CH–CH₃ (2-butene), which itself exists as cis- and trans-isomers

All other choices have only one set of β-hydrogens and so can form only a single alkene.

content:
Both reactions (i) and (ii) give the same compound X as the major product. Identify X

(i). 3-Methylbut-1-ene $+\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$

(ii). Neopentyl alcohol $+\mathrm{HCl}\left(\right.$ anh. $\left.\mathrm{ZnCl}_2\right) \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$
explanation:
The key in both cases is that although you start with a “non‐tertiary” cation, it rearranges (1,2‐shift) to the same tertiary centre, and then Cl⁻ attacks that. In each case the final product is thus the tertiary chloride

(CH₃)₂C–Cl–CH₂–CH₃

which is 2-chloro-2-methylbutane (Option B).

Outline of each mechanism:

1) 3-Methylbut-1-ene + HCl

• Protonation at C-1 gives a secondary cation at C-2.

• A 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent tertiary C (the –CH(CH₃)– centre) moves the positive charge to that carbon, yielding 

    (CH₃)₂C⁺–CH₂–CH₃

• Cl⁻ then attacks, giving (CH₃)₂CCl–CH₂–CH₃.

2) “Neopentyl” alcohol + HCl/ZnCl₂ (Lucas conditions)

• ZnCl₂ activates the –OH, loss of H₂O gives the primary “neopentyl” cation at C-1.

• A 1,2-methyl migration from the adjacent tertiary centre converts it into the identical tertiary cation 

    (CH₃)₂C⁺–CH₂–CH₃

• Cl⁻ attacks to furnish (CH₃)₂CCl–CH₂–CH₃ again.

Hence in both (i) and (ii) the major product X is 2-chloro-2-methylbutane, Option B.

content:
Identify the compound which gives an optically active haloalkane on reaction with $\mathrm{H}_2$ / Ni on heating.

A B C D

Identify the final product formed when Toluene undergoes a series of reactions with reagents given in the order:

(i) $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$ / Sunlight

(ii) $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} / 373 \mathrm{~K}$$

(iii) Acetophenone / $$\mathrm{OH^-}$$ at $$293 \mathrm{~K}$$
Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their reactivity when each of them is reacted with chloroethane / anhydrous AlCl$$_3$$.

Match the names of reactions given in Column I with the appropriate reactions given in Column II.

No. Name of reaction No. Equations representing reactions
(A) Sandmeyer (P) $$
2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{Na} \text { (dry ether) } \rightarrow \text { Diphenyl. }
$$
(B) Fittig (Q) $$
\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHBr}-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{AgF} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHF}-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{AgBr}
$$
(C) Swarts (R) $$
\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{Nal} \text { ( Acetone) } \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{NaBr}
$$
(D) Finkelstein (S) $$
\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{~N}_2+\mathrm{Cl}-+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{HBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{N}_2
$$
explanation:
To match the names of the reactions given in Column I with the appropriate reactions given in Column II, let's analyze each reaction and its equation.

1. Sandmeyer Reaction:

This reaction involves the replacement of the diazonium group with another substituent using copper salts. The correct equation corresponding to this reaction is:

$$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{~N}_2+\mathrm{Cl}-+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{HBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{N}_2$$

2. Fittig Reaction:

The Fittig reaction is similar to the Wurtz reaction, involving the coupling of aryl halides using sodium in dry ether to form biaryl compounds. The appropriate equation for this reaction is:

$$2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{Na} \text { (dry ether) } \rightarrow \text { Diphenyl. }$$

3. Swarts Reaction:

This reaction involves the exchange of a halogen atom in an alkyl halide for fluorine using a metallic fluoride. The corresponding equation is:

$$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHBr}-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{AgF} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHF}-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{AgBr}$$

4. Finkelstein Reaction:

The Finkelstein reaction involves the halogen exchange where an alkyl halide reacts with a halide salt in acetone to form a new alkyl halide. The relevant equation is:

$$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{Nal} \text { ( Acetone) } \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{NaBr}$$

Now, let's match them:

(A) Sandmeyer = (S)

(B) Fittig = (P)

(C) Swarts = (Q)

(D) Finkelstein = (R)

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{S} \; \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{P} \; \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{Q} \; \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{R} $$

Hence, Option A is the correct answer.

content:
Arrange the compounds $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$ and $$\mathrm{D}$$ in the increasing order of their reactivity towards $$\mathrm{S_N}1$$ reaction.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl} \\ & \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl} \\ & \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{Cl} \\ & \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Cl} \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
To determine the order of reactivity of the given compounds $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$$ and $$\mathrm{D}$$ towards an $$\mathrm{S_N}1$$ reaction, we need to consider the stability of the carbocation intermediate that forms when each compound undergoes the reaction. The more stable the carbocation, the more reactive the compound will be towards the $$\mathrm{S_N}1$$ mechanism.

Let's analyze each compound:

1. $$\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{Cl}$$: The carbocation formed after losing $$\mathrm{Cl}$$ is $$\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CH}_2^+$$, which is a benzyl carbocation. Benzyl carbocations are highly stabilized by resonance with the phenyl ring.

2. $$\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{Cl}$$: The carbocation formed after losing $$\mathrm{Cl}$$ is $$\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5^+$$. This is a phenyl carbocation, which is very unstable since the positive charge cannot be delocalized on the aromatic ring effectively.

3. $$\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{Cl}$$: The carbocation formed after losing $$\mathrm{Cl}$$ is $$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}^+$$, which is a vinyl carbocation. Vinyl carbocations are extremely unstable because the positive charge lies on an $$sp^2$$ carbon, making them very high in energy.

4. $$\mathrm{D}=\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Cl}$$: The carbocation formed after losing $$\mathrm{Cl}$$ is $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2^+$$, which is a primary carbocation. Primary carbocations are more stable than vinyl carbocations but much less stable compared to benzyl carbocations.

Based on the stability of the carbocations, the order of reactivity towards the $$\mathrm{S_N}1$$ reaction is:

$$\mathrm{B} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{D} < \mathrm{A}$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option C

$$\mathrm{B} < \mathrm{D} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{A}$$

content: Identify the products C, D and F formed in the following sets of reactions.

Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of reactivity towards electrophilic substitution reaction.

(I) Chlorobenzene

(II) Nitrobenzene

(III) Benzene

(IV) Isopropylbenzene

Arrange the following in the order of increasing reactivity towards $$\mathrm{SN}_{\mathrm{2}}$$ reactions.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{CCH}_2 \mathrm{Br}(\mathrm{P}) ; & \\\mathrm{CH}_3\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_3 \mathrm{Br}(\mathrm{Q}) ; &\\ \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)-\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}(\mathrm{R}) ; & \\\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{CHCH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}(\mathrm{S}) \end{array}$$
explanation:
Let's analyze each compound, focusing on how the structure around the carbon bonded to bromine (the electrophilic center) affects its $$\mathrm{S_N2}$$ reactivity:

$$\mathbf{P: \left(CH_3\right)_3CCH_2Br}$$

Although the $$\mathrm{CH_2Br}$$ group is technically primary, it is attached to a very bulky $$\left(CH_3\right)_3C-$$ group.

This "neopentyl" arrangement makes backside attack very difficult due to the steric hindrance of the adjacent tertiary center.

Thus, P is very slow in $$\mathrm{S_N2}$$ reactions.

$$\mathbf{Q: CH_3(CH_2)_3Br}$$

This is a straight-chain (n-butyl) bromide with very little steric hindrance.

The nucleophile can easily access the carbon bearing the leaving group.

Therefore, Q is highly reactive toward $$\mathrm{S_N2}$$.

$$\mathbf{R: CH_3CH_2-CH(CH_3)-CH_2Br}$$

The $$\mathrm{CH_2Br}$$ is primary; however, the carbon directly attached (the beta carbon) is substituted as $$CH(CH_3)$$.

This beta branching hinders the approach of the nucleophile more than in a straight chain.

So, R reacts slower than Q, but it is still more reactive than severe cases like neopentyl.

$$\mathbf{S: \left(CH_3\right)_2CHCH_2CH_2Br}$$

The bromine is attached to a primary carbon and here the bulky isopropyl group $$\left(CH_3\right)_2CH-$$ is one carbon further away from the reactive center (at the gamma position).

Because the bulky group is not directly next to the reaction center, the steric hindrance is less severe than in compound R.

Therefore, S is more reactive than R, though still not as reactive as the unhindered n-butyl bromide Q.

Arranging these in order of increasing $$\mathrm{S_N2}$$ reactivity (from least reactive to most reactive):

$$\mathbf{P < R < S < Q}$$

This corresponds to Option C.

content: Choose the incorrect statement from the following.

options.content: Carbon tetrachloride is used as a preservative for anatomical specimens
Freon-12 acts as a propellant in aerosol sprays
Dichloromethane is used as a solvent in drug industry
DDT is useful to protect crops and to kill mosquitoes that spread malaria

explanation:
The incorrect statement is Option A. Here's a brief overview of why:

Option A: Carbon tetrachloride is not used as a preservative for anatomical specimens. In reality, preservative solutions for anatomical specimens typically contain formalin (formaldehyde solution) or alcohol, not carbon tetrachloride, which is known more for its solvent properties and toxicity.

Option B: Freon-12 has historically been used as a propellant in aerosol sprays.

Option C: Dichloromethane (methylene chloride) is indeed used as a solvent in the pharmaceutical (drug) industry.

Option D: DDT was used both to protect crops and to control mosquitoes that spread malaria (although its use is now heavily regulated or banned in many countries due to environmental concerns).

Thus, Option A is the incorrect statement.

content: The reaction $$\mathrm{ArN_2^ + C{l^ - }\buildrel {Cu/HCl} \over \longrightarrow ArCl + {N_2} + CuCl}$$ is called as
explanation: The given reaction is Gattermann reaction. In this reaction, chlorine or bromine can be introduced in the benzene ring by treating the diazonium salt solution with corresponding halogen acid in the presence of copper powder.

content: Benzene reacts with chlorine in sunlight to give a final product
explanation: Benzene reacts with chlorine in sunlight to give a final product gammaxene.

content: An alkyl halide reacts with alcoholic ammonia in a sealed tube, the product formed will be

options.content: a mixture of all the three

explanation: When an alkyl halide reacts with alcoholic ammonia in a sealed tube, the product formed will be mixture of primary, secondary and tertiary amine.
